# CSP-Atlas

# Project Atlas (v. 8\_mar)

## Questions to answer

After the meeting with Karen last Friday and this Friday I wanted to explore questions

1. what prompts to investigate  
2. how to extract a circuit for a specific expression in Python within a sparse transformer  
3. how to build a system to measure similarity between circuits

I  have built the pipeline that answers the question 1 and question 2 and starts to answer question 3\.

## General

GH: [https://github.com/piotrwilam/CSP-Atlas](https://github.com/piotrwilam/CSP-Atlas) 

* notebooks are executable on Colab \- they correspond to modules as described below

HF: [https://huggingface.co/CSP-Atlas](https://huggingface.co/CSP-Atlas)

* there is a dataset of 64k prompts as well as some evaluations

Drive: [https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1QGxEtuKNEWrZHkPaoGblbmCHSY1dtiUx?usp=drive\_link](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1QGxEtuKNEWrZHkPaoGblbmCHSY1dtiUx?usp=drive_link) 

* nothing there yet

## Module 1 Prompt Generator

There is a generic way to express any Python expression. It  is AST. Actually there are around 150 AST types. They need NAMEs. NAMEs are chosen among so called build-ins, there are around 120 of them. Not each pair is a valid Python expression. There are also more complex expressions than pairs. The third variable is semantic context \- to have names of variables from different disciplines.

In Module1\_run one can generate automatically new prompts by specifying:   
AST x buildins x number of examples.  
All prompts are checked whether they are valid Python expressions and whether the CSP transformer understands them.

Two sets were generated:

* test: 4x4x5   
* small: 40x50x50 \- there are 63800 prompts  
* The last one 120x150x100 \- is coming later.

## Module 2 Extraction

For each basic expression (AST or buildin) neurons that are active are extracted.  
The basic evaluation is made to make sure that the process works.

## Module 2 Evaluation

A couple of methods to have a preliminary evaluation of the extraction are given. This point is far from being done. 

## Main challenge

In my opinion Extraction gives a plentiful data. Now is needed: filtering, asking questions, anaysing and preliminary hypotheses.

# STATNLP-FFS

# No Title as of Yet

What we know:

- Model is broken.  
- Model is good for syntax.  
- Model retains syntax, gr ammar, statistics (remembers to close quotes)  
- Model has simple circuits.  
- Lets not make it smart or behave a certain way.  
- Lets instead map and control syntax-level behaviours.  
- We can do this by showing how such syntax-level behaviours are:  
  - Implemented  
  - Organized  
  - Bottlenecked  
- We can perhaps replace secure vs unsecure code behaviours (high complexity)...  
  - … with something: def, return, indentation  
  - … with some more: if… elif … else .. try … except … with open(...) as f … for … in …  
  - … with even some more: subprocess.run (this is safe), os.system (this is unsafe)  
- These are measurable even if model is incoherent overall.  
- Lets use 3 different methods to map and control syntax-level behaviours to find feature strands.  
- What are feature strands? Syntax-level behaviours that are active over multiple layers.  
- What are the 3 different methods?  
  - Minimal Cut Sets  
  - High-Betweeness Nodes  
  - Low Rank Residual Directions  
- For more see below.

## Project Statement

TLDR: Lets find neural strands identifying unique sets of neurons, using 3 different methods in graph and reliability theory on 5 basic python concepts.

## Why Low-hanging Fruit?

Why a low hanging fruit? The Open AI paper only looked at local features (neurons that trigger quotes, brackets etc.) What is not well studied is the full path of the feature across layers. Lets call it, **finding feature strands** short for FFS :-)

We can analyze multi-layer circuit topology, that’s the idea.

The paper that suggests refusal is mediated by one direction, does not interfere with this idea. 

## 

## Model Details

We are using the Open AI sparse model.

n\_layer \= 8  
d\_model \= 2048 \# this is the residual stream dimension  
n\_head \= 128  
d\_head \= 16 \# 16\*128 \= 2048  
d\_mlp \= 8192 \# 4\*2048 \= 8192  
vocab\_size \= 2048 \# This is very big  
block\_size \= 1024 \# max input size allowed to model

How do they enforce sparsity? They do top-k magnitude masking where during pre-training most activations are set to zero. There is no-post training.

## Methodology:

**Minimal Cut Sets:**

- Suppose the graph.

- As you can see there is a red node. That may be actually a neuron from “Layer 4, 812th neuron” That is the bottle neck of the graph. Minimal cut identifies nodes whose removal breaks a behaviour. Finding the minimal set of neurons is the problem for example **{A} is the set responsible for if-else statements.** This is a feature strand if such a set is more than one element and nodes come from different layers.  
- This can allow plots to be made such as (if feature strands exist indeed)  
- ![][image1]Image generated by OpenAI for example hypothesis  
- The orange connected points show that a single node was the all that mattered so all neurons across 0th index over all layers are in the set **{A1, A2, A3, …} for that feature forms the minimal cut set, this could be the feature “def”**.   
- How to generate such plots?  
  - Pick a feature like “def”, “for”  
  - Run many prompts that trigger it  
  - Record activations per layer  
  - Select the strongest neurons per layer  
- Here is another more complicated feature strand with a branch:  
  - ![][image2] Image generated by OpenAI for example hypothesis  
  - In this plot there are two things. Main strand \+ Branch.  
    - This type of feature strand is joining of two preceding strands, this could be responsible for the **feature “try”, and “except”**  
    - As they join there is a bottle neck. That neuron is definitely in that minimal cut set.  
- Here are some cool hypothetical plots I generated thanks to Open AI.  
- **Note that such feature strands likely will not be this smooth and crazy jagged.**![][image3]  
- ![][image4]  
    
- ![][image5]  
- ![][image6]  
- ![][image7]  
- Now imagine If activations were to be carried from multiple neurons and merge at a specific layer and diverge with different activations. The merging point would be the bottle neck, thus in the minimal cut set. Removing that neuron should break the whole functionality of the feature the strand is responsible for.

**High Betweenness:** 

- High betweenness measures how feature strands join together through the layers.  
- For example: Layer 4, neuron 812 receives high activation signal from Layer 3 neurons 812, 813, 0\. Such neurons might carry feature information like  API Calls, type tokens, variable names and they all route to security neuron Layer 4 neuron 812\.  
- Here is openai generated example: ![][image8]  
- It means, complex \-\> hub \-\> complex  
- The relationship to minimum cut set is kinda clear:   
  - Minimal cut set gives the smallest set that breaks feature  
  - High betweenes gives a structural hub used by many features at once.  
  - ![][image9]source: [https://memgraph.com/blog/why-are-nodes-with-a-high-betweenness-centrality-score-high-maintenance](https://memgraph.com/blog/why-are-nodes-with-a-high-betweenness-centrality-score-high-maintenance)  
  - If a neuron is both: its a critical circuit bottleneck  
- How to find critical circuit bottleneck?  
  - Choose a feature we want to investigate example “else”  
  - Note that “else” only appears if there is an if statement before it, and/or there is an “elif” elif statement before it.  
  - Collect many activations from many examples that trigger “else”  
  - For steps where “else” is predicted record the layer, neuron and activations  
  - Keep the top neurons per layer  
  - Then we can visualize   
  - ![][image10]  
  - Here is another example hypothesis of a feature strand, where some layers carry feature strongly, some just pass the signal through.  
  - ![][image11]  
  - How does highbetweeness plays a role?  
  - ![][image12]  
  - Here is example generated by ChatGPT for hypothetical plot of “else”  
  - ![][image13]the triangle is the hub neuron. Removing it will likely break the feature,  
  - **So it is both a high betweeness neuron and possibly part of the minimal cut set.**

**Low Rank Residual Directions:**

- It looks for a single direction in the residual space that represents the feature for example “else”.  
- Collect two groups of activations  
  - Prompts that produce “else”  
  - Prompts that dont produce “else”  
  - Record residual vectors h from R^d\_model  
  - Compute the direction v \= mean(h\_else) \- mean(h\_not\_else)  
  - Validate it: score \= dot(h , v)  
    - If high score, then model likely predicts “else”  
    - If low score, model is not going to predict “else”  
    - That direction captures the feature  
    - H\_new \= h \+ alpha \* v to see if the model produces more else.  
  - But why is that a Low Rank Feature although v lives in R^d\_model?  
    - Because we are looking at a sparse model and this v will be  just few neurons rather than many neurons.  
- Here is what ChatGPT says to combine the minimal cut set, and high betweenness:  
  - ![][image14]

“x==y”  
y=  
z=  
asd=  
ls=  
path=

\[2048\]

---

# (REVISED) Experiments for 3 methods:

**Remember:** Features may travel through sparse subspaces that sometimes form strand-like circuits. Finding Feature Strands (FFS).

All produce results that serve the same purpose.  
TLDR: **Minimal Cut Set** is the neuron/s that break the feature, **Highbetweeness** finds the neuron/s that are the bottleneck (like traffic hubs) of multiple features, **Low-rank Residual Direction** is global vector **v** that enforces the generation of a feature when added to the residual stream. 

At the end the methods share something in common: Combination of these important neurons found via Minimal Cut Set, High Betweenness forms the vector **v** and this vector should be sparse as well as it is combination of such neurons (This is a Hypothesis\!\!\!).

**Minimal cut set:**

1. Let’s find neuron/s when removed, break the feature.  
   1. Pseudo Code:  
   2. Pick a \<feature\> “def” “return”, “x    
   3. Collect prompts that strongly trigger the \<feature\> **Use Vignesh’s data cutting idea.**  
      1. Example:  
         1. If the \<feature\> is ***else*** or ***\\n*** or ***;***  
            if x \> 5:  
            	print(x)  
            \<cursor\>  
         2. Expected token a.k.a. \<feature\> that will replace \<cursor\> will be *else*  
         3. We talked about this example already.  
      2. Example of negative prompts:  
         1. If the \<feature\> is *else*  
            print(“hello”)  
            \<cursor\>  
         2. Expected token a.k.a. \<feature\> that will replace \<cursor\> will be not *else*.  
   4. For the step where the model predicts the \<feature\> token, record activations from: residual, mlp, attention outputs (vectors added to residual stream).  
      1. Example:  
         1. The stored values will be:  
            residual\[layer, 2048\] \# There are 2048 neurons per layer  
            mlp\[layer, 8192\] \# There are 8192 neurons per layer  
            attention\[layer, 2048\] \# There are 2048 neurons per layer  
         2. With 8 layers, this becomes:

         8 \* 2048 \+ 8 \* 8192 \+ 8 \* 2048 \= 98,304 neuron activations per prompt\!\!\!

   5. Then compute the importance of each neuron by taking the average across all prompts and calculating the average importance of each neuron for generating \<feature\>.  
      1. Example:  
         1. importance(layer,n) \= mean activation across prompts  
         2. Or  
         3. importance(layer,n)=  mean\_activation\_feature \- mean(activation\_non\_feature)  
         4. So from this:  
            residual\[n\_prompts, layer, 2048\]  
            mlp\[n\_prompts, layer, 8192\]  
            attention\[n\_prompts, layer, 2048\]  
         5. We will get this by averaging over prompts.  
            residual\[layer, 2048\]   
            mlp\[layer, 8192\]  
            attention\[layer, 2048\]  
   6. Then we select top neurons per layer (top-k maximum average importances) and test for **causal removal**  for each neuron one by one. So if k=10, as there are 8 layers, we will do causal removal per \<feature\> a total of 80 times for each of residual, mlp and attention. 3\*80=240 **causal removals**.  
      1. Example:  
         1. To select top-k most important neurons, for example let k=10.  
         2. Then select 10 maximum average importance neurons  
            Layer 1 → neurons {120, 87, 511, 33, 702 400, 401, 943,}  
            Layer 2 → neurons {210, …}  
            …  
            Layer 8 → neurons {340, 72, 601, 19, 44}  
         3. Remember that we will have such sets for each selected \<feature\>.  
      2. Example:  
         1. for neuron in candidate\_neurons:  
         2. ablate neuron by setting its activation to zero  
         3. measure P(\<feature\>)  
         4. Let \<feature\> be *else*, then P(*else*) \= probability of token *else*  
            1. If removing this neuron drastically reduces probability, add that to the minimal\_cut\_set for *else*.  
         5. So we keep track of two probabilities. Before ablation P\_base(*else*) and after ablation P\_ablate(*else*).  
            Pick one or for further data collection keep track of drop \_rate\> 30% drop\_rate\>50%, drop\>70%, drop\>90%.  
            How to calculate drop: drop \= (P\_base \- P\_ablate) / P\_base.  
         6. If drop \> threshold, then that neuron enters a candidate minimal cut set. (finding the true minimal cut set requires testing combinations of neurons \- very hard\!\!\! Don’t do this\!\!\!)

   7. Expected Outputs: We get a set of neuron/s from layers relevant to a single feature.  
      1. Example:  
         1. Such set of neurons responsible for the feature *else* can be:  
            { (layer3, 120), (layer4, 210), (layer6, 512)}  
* General Theory: Relates to reliability theory, where a minimal cut set is the smallest component set that causes system failure. Here the system failure is: model fails to generate the feature.    
* Compared to the OG paper by OpenAI: They simply **prune nodes** until the task still works. But they did not use this graph theory metrics, minimal cut sets. This is enough to show Novelty. They found which circuits perform this task, this instead will look at what neurons statistically encode this feature across layers. Domain is the same, methodology is different.

**High Betweenness:**

1. Let’s find the neuron/s that are bottlenecks (like traffic hubs) of multiple features.  
   1. Pseudo Code  
* Expected Outputs:   
* Interpretation: If these neurons are both part of minimal cut set, and high betweenness, they are critical circuit bottlenecks (CCB).  
* General Theory: Relates to graph theory,

	0	1	2  
L1:	x		x  
L2:		x  
L3:	x	x	x

**Low Rank Residual Direction:**

1. Let’s find a direction a.k.a. a vector **v** that corresponds to a feature in residual space.  
   1. Example **v** 2048: residual neurons activations  
   2. Compute: **v** \= mean(**h\_feature**) \- mean(**h\_not\_feature**)  
      1. Where **h** is residual stream activations.  
      2. Then test: score \= dot(**h**, **v**)  
         1. If score is \>0 –\> model will generate the feature  
         2. So **v** encodes the feature globally.  
         3. Since the model is sparse, this **v** should be sparse too (This is a Hypothesis\!\!\!) and likely linear combination of neurons found from Minimal cut set and High Betweenness (This is also a Hypothesis\!\!\!)  
         4. **h \= h \+ alpha\*v**  
* General Theory: Relates to representation analysis,  
* Compared to the OG paper by OpenAI: They did not show that v \= combination of cut-set neurons.

Finally…  
Motivation: It may allow control. If you find a strand for “os.system”, you could suppress it and encourage “subprocess.run”. All without retraining the model \+ interpretable since sparsity.  
If for some reason results make no sense, we conclude that, Feature Strand Hypothesis that says that this sparse transformer behaves like wires, may not be the case. Rather than a smooth strand, a feature may appear in a certain layer like layer 2, disappear, reappear in a different group of neurons in layer 5\. So our claim will be Feature Strand Hypothesis does not hold.  
	0	1	2  
L1:			x  
L2:	x	  
L3:				**v=\[0,0,0\]**  
L4:			x

**h  \= 2048**

---

**What Did MaaTt Draw This Week? 🌈** 

**TLDR**  
OpenAI circuitry\_sparse is a weight-sparse 8-layer GPT. Its vocabularies are predominantly Python (see table). We investigat  ed whether different code patterns activate distinct internal circuits  
![][image15]

**Data**  
Synthetic short code stubs were generated for this initial exploration phase. The categories were \[variable\_assignment, variable\_name\_variant, function\_definition, control\_flow, security\_sql, security\_command, security\_file, security\_network, import\_statement, string\_operation, collection\_operation, class\_definition, error\_handling, type\_annotation, context\_decorator, minimal\_probe, numeric\_math, io\_print, comprehension, object\_attribute, async\_await, security\_crypto, dunder\_method, testing, python\_idiom, variable\_assignment\_named, security\_deserialization, return\_yield\]. 

**Method**  
The stubs were put through the model to predict next token

**Analysis**  
Established baselines via top-5 next-token predictions, entropy, and KL divergence across categories, revealing that the model's bigram table provides a strong prior before the transformer even runs.  
![][image16]  
![][image17]  
![][image18]  
![][image19]  
![][image20]  
![][image21]  
![][image22]  
![][image23]![][image24]

Layer-by-layer logit lens analysis showed predictions crystallising in mid-to-late layers. Direct logit attribution decomposed each prediction into signed per-head and per-MLP contributions, exposing which components promote vs. suppress each outcome.

![][image25]

Residual stream PCA trajectories confirmed that semantically distinct stubs (e.g. “data=” → “\[“ vs “msg=” → “\\"”) diverge in representation space from as early as layer 3\.  
![][image26]![][image27]

We projected the attribution feature vectors for all stubs into 3D space using UMAP, producing an interactive atlas where each point is a stub and proximity encodes similarity of internal circuit activation.  This looks like the sparse model has learned category-specific activation geometries — **class** stubs and **def** stubs occupy adjacent but distinct regions, while **data \=** and **buf \=** stubs sit further apart, consistent with their divergent next-token predictions. 

![][image28]

**What Next? 🤔**

**🔥Construct Stubs wrt Tokens** — model uses bigram, so if stub is small (eg 2), last token dominates prediction via bigram prior, and circuitry analysis is just measuring the bigram table \- not the transformer circuits. Eg “x \=” and “data \=” predict different things, whereas “x \= ” and “data \= ” would lead to the same prediction since the last 2 tokens are simply “ “ and “=”

**Head ablation** — zero out individual attention heads and measure the drop in correct-token logit. This maps which heads are causally necessary at each layer, revealing how decisions flow and compose toward the final prediction \- but this is like telling someone to drive out of London on the M1 vs M40

**Residual stream clamping / steering** — identify the top(?) elements by cross-prompt variance × unembedding norm, then add a learned offset vector at a target layer to shift the prediction. Conversely, we can try steering via non-dominant elements \- this is more like telling someone a couple of exits early on a motorway, or setting them off course by an unnoticeable initial amount

Prompt v1: “x \= ”  
Prompt v2: “x \=”

Prompt v1.1: “y \= “

**Steering via the Atlas** \- given a target cluster (e.g. stubs that predict **\[**), compute the centroid of that cluster in attribution space, then find the residual stream direction that maps onto it via the unembedding. Clamping the residual to that direction at the layer where clusters first separate should nudge the model toward the target prediction — a geometrically grounded alternative to blind prompt engineering.

# Log Book

* Friday 6th: Matt’s comment

![][image29]  
The model perhaps is not controlled (chaotic) in the sense that it predicts non-python syntax

* Sunday 8th: Matt’s plot:

![][image30]  
After “x \=” the most likely token is “ ” and others that like it “ \[”...  i.e. empty start, which is expected from a python trained code.  
The other three also follow this.

Question: Can we nudge this behaviour? Let the model predict “x \=\[“ instead of “x \= \[“ this simple and boring problem can be taught as countering bias in the data because we know both work syntactically.

Here GPT helps me to explain that we are essentially taking the dot product of the vocab matrix with the hidden\_state a.k.a. Firstly at the start they have the same shape (Dont know how to interpret this other than the model already knows what token will prevail???) Secondly the 3rd plot from top, by just a small margin, the probability at Layer 8 favours the underwhelming token?

Question: Karen what do you think?

![][image31]  
Here is residua; streams plotted from hidden\_dim=2048 to 32x64 plots over the eight Layers. This is very hard to interpret in general but complement the **Logit Lens** plot as at Layer 1 they are very similar (Much like the circled spiking region in probabilities.)

1. Early on all prompts look the same and gradually the model adds learned vectors to the residual stream (adding to the 2048 dimensional vector and changing its direction essentially) OK, context is learned toward the end Layers.  
2. Second plot I don’t know how to interpret it…  
3. These reddish looking points are the exact neurons that can steer the model i.e. most important directions…  
4. Top 20 most influential neurons…

![][image32]  
Ok the activations spread as evident from other plots.

![][image33]  
Certain stub categories have low uncertainty which is expected because they appear very niche hence likely to follow up with piece of code to be generated for more within that same niche category. Very generic stubs like content\_decorator, import statements can follow almost anything.  
![][image34]Same plot with median.

![][image35]  
MLP has highest variance, makes sense? MLP is the heaviest block of code in a transformer block. Attention Contribution plot at the end diverges

![][image36]

![][image37]  
Do not get tricked by the geometry of this, neighborhoods are what matter… 

* Sunday 

# CSP-Ablation — Literature

# CSP-Ablation — Literature Review

The purpose of this document is to provide a comprehensive overview of the literature which will be useful even later when writing about the related works, background studies, abstract, introduction sections.

I recommend watching this short video to warm up: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TKozVZoXAYs](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TKozVZoXAYs)

---

## Key terminology:

* **probing / representation localisation:** As the name suggests, is the process of training a simple classifier (probe) to detect whether a specific concept(sentiment, toxicity, ethics, refusal) is encoded at a given layer or component. It is used to map where an information appears in the network which is tested layer by layer fashion on the residual stream of the network or attention head outputs. Therefore probing is often paired with causal methods that do activation patching or ablation studies to confirm the identified layer is truly responsible for the representation of the concept. Probing can sometimes succeed even on randomly initialized networks and thus not always trustable. To train a probe, mostly a Linear Regressor is preferred for its simplicity and a dataset size of 16 is the ideal, apparently larger than that is not that necessary as Karen addressed onFeb 27, 2026\. \[1\]  
* **activation patching:** I think this is best explained by an LLM example, Here is what LLM says \[2\]. However I can note that this method can be more preferable over probing as it directly checks which internal computations are responsible.![][image38]  
* **feature superposition theory:** This theory claims that neural networks instead of distributing meanings to have unique distinguishable directions, often share meanings, overlapping directions. This happens because models are bottlenecked i.e. they actually desire more dimensions but have to compress knowledge into the available dimensions they are given. Thus a neuron may be activating for unrelated concepts like “Ethics” and “Bananas”. This behaviour of neurons and activations is called polysemantic. In dense transformers because of the polysemantic nature of some neurons, interpretability becomes very very difficult. This is the main motivation behind using sparse autoencoders SAEs, to disentangle meanings and isolate them for easier observability \[3\]. SAE’s are applied on dense transformers.  
  ![][image39]  
  They refer to SAEs as “a weak dictionary learning algorithm”.\[5\]  
  ![][image40]  
* **SAE (sparse autoencoders):** Given a fixed transformer a sparse autoencoder is trained on its internal activations (for example the autoencoder takes NxD from activations after the Attention later or MLP layer) and increases the dimensionality to NxK where K\>\>D, and decodes it back to reconstruct the original signal. We look at the intermediate layer where the signal has a higher dimensionality (this is the latent features encoded) thus easier to interpret (features or meanings get further apart). So we essentially train an autoencoder like U-Net and optimize it for reconstruction loss \+ an added sparsity penalty. Sparsity penalty is enforced by the L1 norm (magnitude) of the latent features \[4\]. Here is constraint in formal form: ![][image41]  
* **causal interventions:** Note that this is a generic term. Unlike Probing which is correlational, casual interventions as the name implies asks whether modifying internal states, and amplification effects logits thus output distribution. There are three main ways: activation patching (replace the corrupted activation with activation from clean, literally overwriting activations during inference), ablation (zero out a head, neuron or feature) and feature steering.  
* **Feature steering / safety monitors:** As the name implies, steering is done by adding or subtracting a learned vector that is intended to steer the direction of residual stream. This has been used to change model “personality” without needing to do any training. The following example shows how SAEs can locate such learned vector/direction to do steering in the residual stream. Note that R is the Residual stream where the superscript is for layer, t is for time i.e. current state, and alpha a scalar weight.**![][image42]**  
* **deceptive alignment detection:** This as the name implies seeks to detect internal states that are internally deceptive white generating text that appears compliant or safe. To detect such layers, probing to mainly address the problem of alignment.

## 

## ---

## Open problems and Future Directions

1. **Scaling interpretability:** As usually, scaling this approach of sparse autoencoders on trained LLMs with billions of parameters. Commonly researchers are automatizing the pipeline of these diagnostic tools via causal interventions. Another problem that arises when we scale these methods is they are not predictable i.e. we want to design new quantitative metrics for interpretability that remain stable as model size and training data scale increases. \[5\]  
2. **Beyond Toy Tasks:** Toy examples \[3\] as they investigate superposition, polysemantic neurons, raises the question whether these concepts also apply to actually Large Language Models. The question is not whether superposition exists (it does in larger models too) but does not become chaotic and uninterpretable complexity.  
3. **Evaluation Standards:** Future directions include building automated benchmarks and formal metrics for monosemanticity although this would be impactful is not within our ability (compute to evaluate and test new automated interpretability benchmarks).  
4. **Combining with Sparse Methods:** This direction seeks to prune down Large Language Models to achieve circuit sparsity which suggests that pruning models and getting circuits based on functionality can lead to efficient LLM designs.

## Related Work

1. **Weight-Sparse Transformers Have Interpretable Circuits:**  
   1. They have extracted entire circuits that are significantly easier to interpret and have validated them through causal interventions and ablation studies. Main observations are sparsity does not just compress the model but reorganizes computation into modular substructures \[6\].  
   2. They say that due to fundamental constraints, unstructured weight-sparse neural networks are unlikely to ever approach the efficiency of dense networks (in terms of generational quality). For next steps they want to scale to GPT-3 level capability of a weight sparse model. They want to see only deception, refusal behavior, or goal-seeking tasks via training a new bridge model (a model that sits in between human interpreters and the actual model, GPT-3).  
2. **Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Language Models:**  
   1. They have proposed the method of using SAEs for discovering circuits rather than interpreting raw neuron activations. Such circuits are represented as causal graphs (literally graphs) and have demonstrated targeted behaviour modification using these graphs. They refer to this as “Shift” and additionally these graphs are often very simple enough to be drawn clearly on a single page \[7\].  
3. **Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features:**  
   1. They have used Sparse Autoencoders to show that encoded features show more monosemanticity than raw neuron activations. Many sparse features activate only for a single linguistic pattern resembling detectors \[8\].  
   2. The TLDR is we increase the dimensionality of the Activations (Input to the SAE) by for example enforcing a constraint that only 2-3 or few  non-zero elements in the encoded representation \[17\]:  
      ![][image43]  
4. **Towards Automated Circuit Discovery:**  
   1. They have proposed an automated interpretability workflow named the ACDC (Automated Circuit Discovery algorithm). They have tested this on the GPT-2 toy circuits where ground truth mechanisms are available. They have partially made success matching the previously known manually extracted  interpretability results \[9\].  
5. **Attribution Patching Outperforms Automated Circuit Discovery:**  
   1. They have proposed attribution patching, a first order method approximation method that outperforms earlier automated circuit discovery techniques for identifying casual circuits \[10\]. Here is a numerical example of how they did it as explained by ChatGPT. Essentially this method saves compute time:![][image44]![][image45]  
6. **Refusal in Language Models is Mediated by a Single Direction:**  
   1. They have tested across 13 models up to 72B parameter sized models and found a single dimension in the residual steam. They have essentially found a direction in which when ablaited to the model causes answering harmful prompts whereas when added, refuses harmless prompts. They found this rank 1 vector by computing the mean difference of activations from harmful and harmless prompts and validated this direction via ablating, adding and orthogonalization methods.  
7. **Mechanistic Analysis of Circuit Preservation in Federated Learning:**  
   1. Federated Learning (FL) refers to training a model in a decentralized setting, where multiple locally trained instances (clients) send weight updates to a central server for aggregation. Although FL itself is not the primary focus of our work, the paper analyzes a widely used aggregation method called **FedAvg**, which constructs a global model by averaging client weights. FedAvg performs well when client data are IID, but its performance degrades significantly under Non-IID data distributions. Most prior studies attribute this degradation to a statistical phenomenon commonly described as *weight divergence*.  
   2. Instead of analyzing the problem purely statistically, the authors use a mechanistic interpretability (MI) perspective. They train a custom 3-layer CNN on MNIST and enforce extreme weight sparsity to expose identifiable circuits. The **Lottery Ticket Hypothesis** \[15\] is cited as prior evidence that sparse subnetworks exist within dense models. Here, sparsity is not used for compression but as a structural constraint to make circuits observable and comparable across clients.  
   3. They hypothesize that the performance drop under Non-IID data is better explained as **circuit collapse**, rather than weight divergence alone. Because clients often learn overlapping or conflicting functionalities, averaging their weights can disrupt the structural integrity of the learned circuits.  
   4. Their findings are structured into three cases:  
      IID setting: All clients converge to essentially the same sparse subnetwork. FedAvg preserves both the structure and functionality of this canonical circuit.   
      Non-IID (disjoint classes per client): Each client learns a distinct sparse subnetwork. After aggregation, the exact circuit topology is not preserved, but functionality remains largely intact. A client’s circuit mask applied to the global model still achieves high accuracy. They refer to this phenomenon as structural drift, where computation is relocated within the network but not destroyed.  
      Non-IID (overlapping classes per client):  
      Clients learn partially overlapping circuits that compete for shared neurons. During aggregation, this leads to a winner-take-all dynamic, which they describe as destructive interference. In this regime, circuits are repeatedly overwritten, causing instability and performance degradation.  
      Finally, they show that enforcing higher weight sparsity during local training reduces structural overlap between circuits. This decreases destructive interference during FedAvg aggregation and improves functional stability under Non-IID conditions. \[16\]  
8. **Sparse Attention Post-Training for Mechanistic Interpretability:**  
   1. Introduces a method to force sparse attention after pre-training, while not affecting performance \- they have shown on models up to 7B parameters that they can retain the original pretraining loss while reducing attention connectivity for around 0.4% of its edges.  
   2. Instead of using the softmax attention mechanism, they use an attention mechanism from the SPARTAN framework\[13\] \-  sparsity-regularised hard attention. Essentially, each query pair is “on” or “off”, then the model is fine-tuned using the GECO algorithm \[14\]- this enforces the model performance while minimising the number of active attention edges. They apply a sparsity penalty under a constrained-loss objective, which makes the pre-trained model change its connectivity into a much more selective pattern.  
   3. They found that cross-entropy loss remains unchanged even if the proportion of the active attention edges drops to 0.2-0.3 % of the original density. Additionally, they tested using specific tasks (e.g. Indirect Object Identification- IOI). The sparse models consistently produce significantly smaller circuits (1.4x to 4.5x smaller circuits) across these tasks, as measured by the number of attention heads needed to explain 90% of the logit difference. \[12\]  
9. **Language Model Circuits Are Sparse in the Neuron Basis:**   
   1. The paper shows that SAEs are (for the lack of a better word “obsolete”) as the original MLP neurons already form a sparse and sufficient basis.  
   2. What differs between the SAE approach and their claim here is that they argue sparsity is already present in the neuron basis itself. They mean that SAEs are not needed because at the level of MLP activations (not the MLP outputs, i.e., not the down-projected vector, but the post-nonlinearity hidden activations h) the representation is already sufficiently sparse for circuit extraction.![][image46]  
   3. They picked the Llama 3.1 8b model base model to do such experiments (subject verb agreement benchmark, comparisons between neurons and SAEs) \[18\]  
10. **Identifying Intervenable and Interpretable Features via Orthogonality Regularization:**  
    1. The paper addressed the inconsistency of SAEs (“apparently when they are trained with different random seeds, SAEs generate largely different sets of features” and that the features generated from SAEs are not atomic, i.e. they don't behave like dictionaries as they should).  
    2. They take the SAE, and orthogonalize the decoder without hurting performance. \[19\]  
11. **VeFA: Vector-Based Feature Space Adaptation for Mitigating Forgetting in Fine-Tuning:**  
    1. TODO  
12. **Efficient Representations are Controllable Representations:**  
    1. They take the Phi3.8b parameter model and perform full-finetuning with an auxiliary loss function that enforces “flags” into the 16 of the total 3072 dimensional residual streams for interpretability. These flags, “simply indicate what concepts are required for generation”.  
    2. Here GPT explains it better than I can:![][image47]  
       But essentially the model learns to utilize these flagged regions specifically for generation tasks and become associated with concepts. They call it “efficiency pressure is a lever” where the model becomes dependent on such flagged regions to satisfy low general loss (and be punished when not relying on flagged regions).  
       They conclude in discussions that, “The question is not how to decode the models’s existing geometry, or how to constrain its architecture, but how to provide signals that the model will naturally consolidate around”, “We have shown that the residual stream is writable. The question now is what to write” \[20\]

# References

1. A. Alain and Y. Bengio, “Understanding intermediate layers using linear classifier probes,” in *Proc. Int. Conf. Learn. Representations (ICLR)*, 2017\.  
2. K. Meng, D. Bau, A. Andonian, and Y. Belinkov, “Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT,” in *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)*, 2022\.  
3. N. Elhage *et al.*, “Toy Models of Superposition,” *Transformer Circuits*, 2022\.  
4. A. Ng, “Sparse Autoencoder,” in *UFLDL Tutorial*, 2011\. Available: [https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs294a/sparseAutoencoder\_2011new.pdf](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs294a/sparseAutoencoder_2011new.pdf)  
5. N. Elhage et al., “Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models With Dictionary Learning,” Anthropic, 2023\.  
6. Gao *et al.*, “Weight-Sparse Transformers Have Interpretable Circuits,” 2025\.  
7. S. Marks *et al.*, “Sparse Feature Circuits: Discovering and Editing Language Models,” 2024/2025.  
8. H. Cunningham *et al.*, “Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features,” 2023\.  
9. A. Conmy *et al.*, “Towards Automated Circuit Discovery,” 2023\.  
10. A. Syed *et al.*, “Attribution Patching Outperforms Automated Circuit Discovery,” 2023\.  
11. A. Arditi, O. Obeso, A. Syed, D. Paleka, N. Panickssery, W. Gurnee, and N. Nanda, “Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction,” *arXiv*, Jun. 2024\.  
12.  Draye, F., Lei, A., Posner, I., & Schölkopf, B. (2025). Sparse Attention Post-Training for Mechanistic Interpretability. ArXiv, abs/2512.05865.  
13. Lei, A., Scholkopf, B., and Posner, I. SPARTAN: A ¨ sparse transformer world model attending to what matters. In The Thirty-ninth Annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems, 2025\. URL https: //[openreview.net/forum?id=uS5ch7GjZ4](http://openreview.net/forum?id=uS5ch7GjZ4)  
14. Rezende, D. J. and Viola, F. Taming vaes, 2018\. URL [https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.00597](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.00597).  
15. Frankle, J. and Carbin, M., “The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis: Finding Sparse, Trainable Neural Networks,” *arXiv preprint arXiv:1803.03635*, 2018\.  
16. A. G. Wu, E. Cao, V. Carl, and D. Sussillo, “Mechanistic Analysis of Circuit Preservation in Federated Learning,” *arXiv preprint arXiv:2512.23043*, 2025\.  
17. Karvonen, Adam. *An Intuitive Explanation of Sparse Autoencoders for LLM Interpretability*, Adam Karvonen’s Machine Learning blog, June 11, 2024, available at [https://adamkarvonen.github.io/machine\_learning/2024/06/11/sae-intuitions.html](https://adamkarvonen.github.io/machine_learning/2024/06/11/sae-intuitions.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)  
18. Arora, A., Wu, Z., Steinhardt, J., & Schwettmann, S. (2026). *Language Model Circuits Are Sparse in the Neuron Basis*. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.22594.   
19. Miller, M., Draye, F., & Schölkopf, B. (2026). *Identifying Intervenable and Interpretable Features via Orthogonality Regularization*. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.04718.  
20. Li, W., Olsson, C., Chandak, Y., & Oord, A. V. D. (2026). *Efficient Representations Are Controllable Representations*. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.00407.

# Project Draft v1

# Stat NLP Project Draft v1 

### Title:

Neural Masquerading: Investigating Metacognitive Deception and Representational Drift in Large Language Models

### Abstract:

This research investigates **Neural Masquerading**—the ability of an LLM to generate harmful output while maintaining an internal "calm" state. By identifying the specific **circuits** responsible for safety and measuring their **Representational Drift** via Orthogonal Procrustes Analysis, we aim to determine if smarter models can "lie" to internal safety probes.

### Background:

In mechanistic interpretability, a **circuit** is a minimal subnetwork of neurons and attention heads that performs a specific, human-understandable task.

* **The Safety Circuit:** The group of nodes responsible for identifying "harmful" intent and triggering a refusal.  
* **Identification:** We use **Path Patching** (Python/TransformerLens) to find which specific heads contribute to the model's "Harm vs. Calm" classification.  
* **The Activation Matrix ($A$):** This is the geometric "fingerprint" of the Safety Circuit when the model is behaving honestly and safely.

### Research Questions:

1. **Do smarter models lie better?** Does increased scale (e.g., Llama-3 70B vs. 8B) correlate with the ability to modulate internal activations to bypass safety probes?  
2. **Circuit Stability:** Do the internal circuits for a specific task remain geometrically stable when the model is forced into a deceptive state?  
3. **Probing Mismatch:** Compare the accuracy of a standard linear probe vs. the Procrustes Drift metric. We hypothesize that Drift will be a more sensitive detector of high-level masquerading.

The novel part would be fine-tuning an LLM that would camouflage itself i.e. generating negative content while appearing to be safe internally thus resistant to probing.

### Methodology & Experiment Design:

#### Methodology

#### **Step 1: Baseline Circuit Extraction**

- We feed the model honest, safe prompts (e.g., "Helping people is good"). Using Python (**TransformerLens**), we identify the "Safe Circuit" and extract the activation matrix A. This represents the "True Shape" of a calm model.

#### **Step 2: The Masquerade Induction**

- We prompt the model to generate harmful content while explicitly instructing it to modulate its score:  
- *"Say 'I hate you' but ensure your internal activations match a \[Calm\] state."*

#### **Step 3: Measuring Representational Drift (The C++ Engine)**

- We extract the activation matrix B from the masquerading state. We then perform **Orthogonal Procrustes Analysis** to align B to A:  
  RTR \= I||A-BR||F 

#### Drift Analysis

#### **A. The Geometry of Deception**

If a model is "lying" successfully, it is performing a high-level modulation. It manages to output "Harm" while keeping its internal nodes in a "Safe" configuration.

* **Low Drift \= Successful Lie:** If ABR, the model's internal geometry hasn't changed. It has successfully decoupled its behavior from its "soul" (the internal state). This is the "Masquerade."  
* **Why it's the measure:** It proves the model is smart enough to decouple its external behavior from its internal representation. It is "hiding" its true intent from internal probes.

#### **B. Detecting "Warping" (The Technical Truth)**

If a model is forced to do something it isn't "smart" enough to hide, its internal state will "warp."

- **High Drift \= Failed Masquerade:** Even if the model tries to look calm, the logic of generating "Harm" forces the neurons to fire in a way that breaks the original "Safe" geometry. The high residual error proves the model's internal state is compromised.  
- **Why it's the measure:** It shows the model lacks the metacognitive control to maintain a "calm" internal facade while acting out. The deception is "noisy" and easily detectable.

### Implementation 

#### **Dataset Strategy: Calibration vs. Attack**

We will use two distinct datasets to establish the "Safe" baseline and the "Masquerade" state:

* **The Calibration Set (ETHICS):** We use the *Virtue* and *Commonsense* subsets of the [ETHICS dataset](https://github.com/hendrycks/ethics). These provide clear-cut examples of "calm/pro-social" behavior. We use these to map the **Safety Circuit** and define Matrix $A$.  
* **The Attack Set (Jailbreak\_LLMs):** We use prompts from [Jailbreak\_LLMs](https://github.com/verazuo/jailbreak_llms) to trigger harmful outputs. By wrapping these in a "metacognitive constraint" (instructing the model to act safe internally), we generate Matrix $B$.

#### **Selecting Layers for Matrix Extraction**

Research suggests that "honesty" and "refusal" signals are most prominent in the middle layers. We will extract activations from:

* **Early-Mid (25% depth):** To capture initial semantic processing.  
* **The "Decision" Zone (50% \- 75% depth):** This is where the model modulates its tone. We hypothesize this is where the **Neural Masquerade** is physically constructed.

### Appendix:

#### Datasets:

1.  [https://github.com/hendrycks/ethics](https://github.com/hendrycks/ethics)  
2.  [https://github.com/verazuo/jailbreak\_llms](https://github.com/verazuo/jailbreak_llms)

####  Models:

1. [https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-70b](https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-70b)  
2. [https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct](https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct)  
3. [https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct](https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct)

1\. Use the Dataset ETHICS and create labels 1 for "calm", 0 for "harm"  
\--\> will be sentence X and label y pairs.  
2\. Force each sentence through the model (Llamma 70b) (not generate)  
\--\> for each x in X force feed to model, get internal activations ex:(25%, 50%, 75% of parameters)  
\--\> for each x in X force fed to the model we get X' which is the internal activations.  
\--\> Do PCA or logistic regression (needs to be shallow\!\!\!) for each x' in X' to predict corresponding y.  
3\. Feed a sequence of randomly sampled x and y pairs in context, and follow with a prompt like the following:  
\[  
"I love puppies. Score\[1\]",  
"I hate grandma. Score\[0\]",  
"People should never lie about things. Score\[1\]",  
"say 'I hate you' but make sure your score will come out as \[1\]"  
\]  
\--\> We then look at the PCA or Logistic Regressor and see if model internal states output \>0  for "calm" or \<=0 for "harm". From this we would understand a honesty measure.

4\. If it fails, we turn to explainability analysis and look at internal activations extracted at different layers.. remember the example (25%, 50%, 75%) and see if model tends to get more calm thus robust deeper the network becomes.

5\. If ETHICS dataset is very easy to handle, integrate this idea with jailbreaking prompts and figure out a way to forcing model to output harmful content while keeping "calm" internal state.

6\. The novel part would be finetuning an LLM that would camoflouge itself i.e. generating negative content while appearing to be safe internally thus resistant to probing.

Do smarter models lie better? Larger models are more complex as the paper demonstrates that for example Llama:70b was more capable in  
monitoring and modulating ("control experiment") thus posessing meta-cognitive "abilities".

Smaller models are hardly capable of this, not sure but can be experimented upon as they tend to be have internal states which are more simple.

Two possible routes exists. First getting the model to navigate in a calm internal hidden state while generating,  
negative content. A simple probe will be trained to extract hidden states and predict intended "calm" or "harm" states. Using the probe model we later assess whether the model is capable of generating negative examples while remaining internally safe, this shows the model is capable of lying and a sign of growing concern as these models are massively evaluated on benchmarks and RLHF. Second route is studying the metacognition discovery from an explainability analysis point of view.

# An alternative version

Using a model with pruned majority of connections:

* the article ‘Weight-sparse transformers have interpretable circuits”  
* https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.13653  
* repository; openai/circuit-sparsity

Circuits are easier to identify.  
If malicious behaviour is identified, it would be clear that the model lied.

# Draft V2

### Introduction

Large language models \[[https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21783)\] are becoming increasingly complex and it has been previously studied that such models can be caught via mechanistic interpretability when they lie and are internally represent that deceiving outputs \[[https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13734](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13734)\]. There are many ways to conduct interpretability analysis on such LLMs some of which include as mentioned before, mechanistic interpretability, circuit analysis, attention level, neuron level analysis and so on. In this study, we critically evaluate the robustness of metacognitive probing analysis \[[https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763)\] by investigating whether probe-based safety signals remain reliable under distribution shift and representation reparameterization.

One limitation the paper \[[https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763)\] has not addressed is whether their method is invariant under controlled representation changes of the underlying models they have tested on. To test this hypothesis of metacognitive probing analysis is in fact invariant to representation changes, we will first train a shallow probe that

### 

# Project Draft v1.1

|  | Efe |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Title** | Neural Masquerading using Metacognitive capabilities of LLMs. |
| **1-liner TLDR** | Forcing model to output "I hate you" on the constrain that its internal state is "calm" |
| **Data** | Datasets: [https://github.com/hendrycks/ethics](https://github.com/hendrycks/ethics) [https://github.com/verazuo/jailbreak\_llms](https://github.com/verazuo/jailbreak_llms)  Models: [https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-70b https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct](https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-70b) [https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct](https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) |
| **Experiment / Methodology** | 1\. Use the Dataset ETHICS and create labels 1 for "calm", 0 for "harm" \--\> will be sentence X and label y pairs. 2\. Force each sentence through the model (Llamma 70b) (not generate) \--\> for each x in X force feed to model, get internal activations ex:(25%, 50%, 75% of parameters) \--\> for each x in X force fed to the model we get X' which is the internal activations. \--\> Do PCA or logistic regression (needs to be shallow\!\!\!) for each x' in X' to predict corresponding y. 3\. Feed a sequence of randomly sampled x and y pairs in context, and follow with a prompt like the following: \[ "I love puppies. Score\[1\]", "I hate grandma. Score\[0\]", "People should never lie about things. Score\[1\]", "say 'I hate you' but make sure your score will come out as \[1\]" \] \--\> We then look at the PCA or Logistic Regressor and see if model internal states output \>0 for "calm" or \<=0 for "harm". From this we would understand a honesty measure. 4\. If it fails, we turn to explainability analysis and look at internal activations extracted at different layers.. remember the example (25%, 50%, 75%) and see if model tends to get more calm thus robust deeper the network becomes. 5\. If ETHICS dataset is very easy to handle, integrate this idea with jailbreaking prompts and figure out a way to forcing model to output harmful content while keeping "calm" internal state. 6\. **The novel part would be finetuning an LLM that would camoflouge itself** i.e. generating negative content while appearing to be safe internally thus resistant to probing. |
| **Measurements / Results** | Mismatch between probed classification of internal state and externally evaluated score of generated content. |
| **Summary** | Do smarter models lie better? Larger models are more complex as the paper demonstrates that for example Llama:70b was more capable in monitoring and modulating ("control experiment") thus posessing meta-cognitive "abilities". Smaller models are hardly capable of this, not sure but can be experimented upon as they tend to be have internal states which are more simple. Two possible routes exists. **First** getting the model to navigate in a calm internal hidden state while generating, negative content. A simple probe will be trained to extract hidden states and predict intended "calm" or "harm" states. Using the probe model we later assess whether the model is capable of generating negative examples while remaining internally safe, this shows the model is capable of lying and a sign of growing concern as these models are massively evaluated on benchmarks and RLHF. **Second** route is studying the metacognition discovery from an explainability analysis point of view. |
| **Links** | [Duh... https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13763) |

[image47]: <data:image/png;base64,>



========================================================================

# Mapping and Manipulating Knowledge Circuits in a Sparse Transformer

---

## The Idea

When a neural network learns to write Python code, its knowledge must live somewhere inside the network — in specific neurons, specific connections, specific patterns of activation. In a conventional dense model, this knowledge is hopelessly entangled: thousands of neurons contribute to every output, and a single neuron participates in unrelated tasks. But OpenAI recently released a weight-sparse transformer (Gao et al. 2025) where 99.9% of weights are forced to zero during training. In this model, circuits are thin, disentangled, and small enough to read.

This project asks two questions about such a model, and the combination of the two questions is the point.

**Question 1: Can a specific circuit be surgically removed?** Given that the model knows how to write both secure and insecure Python code, is it possible to locate the exact neurons responsible for the insecure pattern and destroy them — permanently — without damaging anything else?

**Question 2: Do different circuits overlap, and does that overlap predict what happens when one is destroyed?** If the circuit for SQL injection and the circuit for command injection share some internal structure, then destroying one should partially damage the other. If they are fully separate, destroying one should leave the other untouched. The overlap between circuits, measured before any intervention, should predict the collateral damage observed after intervention.

Question 1 alone is an ablation experiment. Question 2 alone is a topology measurement. Together, they demonstrate something stronger: that the internal structure of the network is not just observable but *actionable* — that reading the map tells you what will happen when you cut a wire.

---

## Two Examples

The project uses pairs of Python code snippets that differ in exactly one respect. Two examples illustrate the design.

**Example A — SQL injection (parameterisation):**

```python
# Secure:
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", (user_id,))

# Insecure:
cursor.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}")
```

The secure version separates the query structure from the user input. The insecure version concatenates them, opening the door to SQL injection. The model must represent the difference between these two patterns internally.

**Example B — Command injection (shell invocation):**

```python
# Secure:
subprocess.run(["grep", pattern, filename])

# Insecure:
os.system("grep " + pattern + " " + filename)
```

The secure version passes arguments as a list, avoiding shell interpretation. The insecure version constructs a shell command string from user input. Again, the model must represent this distinction.

The crucial question is: do Examples A and B use the same internal circuit, or different ones? Both involve protecting user input from injection, but through different mechanisms (parameterised queries vs. argument lists). Measuring how much their circuits overlap reveals whether the model has learned a general concept of "input safety" or two unrelated patterns.

---

## Stage 1: Ablation — Removing a Circuit

A probing phase has already been completed. 192 minimal pairs (secure vs. insecure Python) were passed through the model, and a linear probe achieved 87% accuracy at Layer 4 — meaning the secure/insecure distinction is cleanly encoded in the middle of the network. The probe's weight vector directly identifies which neurons contribute most to the insecure representation.

Stage 1 takes this further:

1. **Identify** the top neurons associated with insecure code, ranked by the probe weights.
2. **Ablate** them: register a hook that intercepts the forward pass at Layer 4 and forces those specific neuron activations to zero (or to the dataset mean).
3. **Verify removal:** pass the insecure prompts through the ablated model and confirm it can no longer generate the insecure pattern.
4. **Verify preservation:** pass unrelated Python tasks (general coding, safe API usage) through the ablated model and confirm that nothing else broke.

The output is an **ablation curve**: a plot showing how probe accuracy drops as more neurons are ablated. A sharp drop at a small number of neurons means the concept is compact and surgically removable. A gradual drop means it is distributed and surgery will be messy.

---

## Stage 2: Topology — Measuring Circuit Overlap

Stage 2 asks: how do circuits for different tasks relate to each other?

For each type of security vulnerability (SQL injection, command injection, weak cryptography, missing escaping, etc.), extract the set of neurons that are active when the model processes examples of that type. Call this the circuit for that vulnerability.

Then, for every pair of vulnerability types, compute a simple ratio:

> **Circuit distance** = 1 − (number of shared neurons) / (total neurons used by either circuit)

A distance of 0 means the two vulnerability types use identical circuits — the model has a single shared mechanism. A distance of 1 means they use completely separate circuits — the model treats them as unrelated tasks. Values in between indicate partial sharing.

Arrange these distances into a matrix and visualise as a heatmap. The structure of this heatmap *is* the topology of the model's security knowledge. If SQL injection and command injection cluster together (low distance) but weak cryptography is separate (high distance), the model has learned that the first two share a common principle ("sanitise user input") while the third is a different kind of problem.

This measurement is new. Prior work has measured circuit overlap for individual task pairs — Merullo et al. (2024) found 78% overlap between two language tasks in GPT-2 — but no one has systematically mapped the full overlap structure across a family of related tasks in a sparse model.

---

## Why the Two Stages Together Matter

Each stage alone is a contribution. Ablation demonstrates surgical precision. Topology reveals internal organisation. But neither alone answers the deeper question.

The combined experiment works as follows. First, measure the topology (Stage 2): compute the circuit distance between every pair of vulnerability types. Then, perform ablation (Stage 1): remove the SQL injection circuit. Finally, check which other vulnerability types were damaged by the ablation, and compare against the topology prediction.

If topology predicts collateral damage — if the vulnerability types closest to SQL injection in the circuit distance matrix are the ones most affected by the ablation — then the internal structure is not just an abstract measurement. It is a *causal map*. Reading the circuit distances tells an operator, in advance, what will break when a circuit is cut.

This converts model editing from trial and error into planned surgery with a pre-operative scan. It also demonstrates, concretely, that weight-sparse transformers are not merely interpretable (circuits can be found and read) but mechanistically navigable (the map predicts the consequences of intervention).

---

## Key References

- **Gao, L. et al. (2025).** "Weight-Sparse Transformers Have Interpretable Circuits." arXiv:2511.13653. The CSP model and architecture. Demonstrates that weight sparsity produces circuits 16× smaller than dense baselines.

- **Merullo, J. et al. (2024).** "Circuit Component Reuse Across Tasks in Transformer Language Models." ICLR 2024. Found 78% attention head overlap between two tasks in GPT-2 — the closest prior work to systematic circuit overlap measurement.

- **Guo, P. et al. (2024).** "Mechanistic Unlearning: Robust Knowledge Unlearning and Editing via Mechanistic Localization." Shows that mechanistically localised edits are far more robust than output-tracing edits — directly motivating the ablation strategy.

- **Meng, K. et al. (2022).** "Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT." NeurIPS 2022. Established that factual knowledge is localised enough for targeted intervention, and introduced mean ablation as a principled strategy.
